data.name: Introduction
entries: During every quest, adventurers must endure weeks of travel through difficult terrain. Long nights keeping watch by the campfire. Anxious hours waiting for the scouting party's return. In other words, plenty of time for fingers to grow stiff and thoughts to become muddled. What better to ward off such dangers than a craft that requires a dexterous touch, attention to detail, and a bundle of components compact enough to fit into any unenchanted haversack?

I speak, of course, of the timeless art of crochet. Quick to pick up and swift to set down should a wandering monster appear. Easy to learn and endlessly versatile. Lighter than a book and quieter than a ballad. Crochet is perfectly suited to a life of exploration and escapades.

Some may dismiss it as a common craft, unworthy of the grand and epic scope of an adventurer's existence. To my thinking, this quality is what makes it particularly useful to adventurers. It's all too easy, when your life is filled with legendary monsters and powerful magic, to lose your connection to the everyday wonders.

Crochet is an individual pursuit that connects us to a long historical tradition of craftsmanship. Across the realms and throughout the Astral Sea, you'll find the craft appearing time and again. You'll also find it closer to home. Many a time, crochet has been a helpful way to break down barriers. Folks are often eager to start a conversation with a fellow crafter. Talk of stitches and techniques, of favorite patterns and wools, can open doors in unexpected places.

So, yes, pack your tinderbox and torches, your fifty feet of rope, and your weeks of iron rations. But be sure to save space for your hook, your yarn, and a few of these quest-worthy patterns. You may just find that your greatest treasures wind up being the ones crafted by your very own hand.

entries.name: About this Book
entries: You hold in your hands a treasure chest of creative concepts, worthy of inclusion in a gold dragon's hoard. Designed by fellow adventurers, this collection reflects the infinite variety of the realms. Whether you are seeking a simple project to occupy you during a short rest or a complex challenge to improve your skill proficiency, you're sure to find inspiration in these pages.

{@style Dungeons & Dragons Crochet|dnd-font} is divided into three main sections: amigurumi (toy plushies), wearables, and household items. The projects in each section are marked with a difficulty level, progressing from beginner through intermediate and then advanced. If you are entirely new to crochet, I strongly recommend starting with the beginner projects, which will let you practice core crochet techniques. At the end of the book, you'll find a glossary of all the stitches and crochet terms as well as the different types of yarns used in the patterns.

While most patterns can be completed with nothing more than yarn and hook, a few will require additional components. You'll find a complete list of specialty items, such as wire, safety eyes, or tapestry needles, at the start of each project. Be sure to review the detailed steps and requirements for each project and gather your supplies in advance. You do not want to be halfway up the side of White Plume Mountain before realizing that you're short of yarn or other needed materials.

These patterns are intended to be accessible to adventurers of every class and background. There are no techniques that require the use of {@spell mage hand} to wield an additional hook or {@spell unseen servant} to hold stitches into place. If you find it necessary to evoke {@spell bless} or {@spell beacon of hope} while tackling a pattern, you should consider setting it aside for a short while and trying an easier skill-building design first.

The suggested yarns should be available in most material planes. Stitchers in the Feywild, Shadowfell, or other demiplanes should take great care in making substitutions. Yarns made from phase spider silk, chimera fur, or other such magically infused sources often behave in unexpected ways. A variety of complications, from distorted stitches to unplanned interplanar travel, can easily befall the unwary stitcher.
Without further fanfare, I invite you to take up hook and yarn on a quest of your own!

data.name: Amigurumi
entries: Walkway could be better than transforming fearful monsters and vile artifacts into cute and cuddly tokens of delight? That's precisely the magic you'll work with these adorable amigurumi patterns. These figurines are woven through with an enchantment that will appeal to adventurers of all ages and skill levels.

The realms offer an abundance of creatures whose likeness can be captured with yarn and hook. The chosen patterns cover a range of shapes and sizes, from the simple geometry of the {@pattern Gelatinous Cube} to the realm-hopping complexity of a {@pattern Displacer Beast}. After mastering these designs, advanced crafters might even want to try their hand at adapting these patterns to extend their repertoire. For example, other types of dragons might be created by adjusting the head frills and wing shape of the {@pattern Red Dragon} pattern—and changing the yarn color, of course.

Lest you dismiss this section as mere child's play, recall the usefulness of these amigurumi for any monster hunter. Few people have an adventurer's familiarity with the nuances of creature anatomy. Being able to provide a clear visual reference can be useful when determining the details of the threat you're being asked to confront. A {@pattern beholder} with only four eyestalks is merely a {@creature spectator}, for instance—still a threat, but a lesser one. Making sure that you're facing a {@creature panther} and not a {@pattern displacer beast} is well worth the effort of stitching up that pattern!

Indeed, {@creature Volothamp "Volo" Geddarm|ToA|Volo Geddarm} claims to have met a Cormyrian monster hunter who handcrafted one amigurumi to represent each of the dangerous creatures that they had slain. It's worth noting that {@creature Volothamp "Volo" Geddarm|ToA|Volo} was, at the time, selling a set of plushie toys purportedly from this collection, so the anecdote should be taken with a Volo-sized grain of salt. Even so, it illustrates one of the myriad uses an adventurer may come up with for the patterns in this section.

Once completed, you'll find that amigurumi are incredibly handy. They may serve as a cherished companion during solo quests or as a token of appreciation for your quest companions. They can be used to delight children, enhance a bardic performance, or distract a bored sentry. They can even become an offering of thanks to the unseen forces guiding you through your adventures. The choice is all yours.

data.name: Wearables
entries: While no one would be so foolish as to suggest that clothes make the adventurer, the right attire can assist your exploits in many ways. Scarves and hats help keep your focus on your mission instead of the biting winds that hinder your progress over mountains steep and oceans wide. Gloves protect your fingers, keeping them warm and ready when the time comes to let arrows or spells fly. Sturdy bags keep all your gear collected and organized, ready to grab should a secret door or sudden ambush require your quick response.

Beyond their practical application, items of handmade clothing are a delightful way to carry your individual style through long journeys and dark dungeons. Everything you bring on a quest needs to serve a purpose, often more than one. With the ability to make your own unique items, there's no reason to let space constraints dampen your personal flair. By making your own scarves, hats, and gloves, you can avoid those awkward moments when it becomes clear that the entire party stocked up at the same merchant before hitting the road.

In addition, wearable items can help you overcome one of the greatest obstacles faced by fledgling adventurers—namely, persuading skeptical strangers to accept your offers of aid. The glint of dragon scales woven into a scarf or the arcane patterning of a magical bag can help you gain the trust of wary villagers just as quickly as a battle-scarred axe—and with fewer nervous whispers.

They can also help you shore up your supplies or be bartered in exchange for a warm bed for the night. While innkeepers tend to prefer payment in coins, there are many across the realms happy to trade useful items during your journeys.

These patterns have been well-tested and should suit a variety of skill levels, from beginners to expert stitchers. Some are inspired by famous monsters and beloved deities, while others evoke wondrous magical items that any adventurer would be proud to possess. Each one provides a chance to explore different techniques, expand your repertoire, and create a custom piece sure to delight yourself or any lucky recipient!

One useful note: These patterns have been scaled for the average humanoid and should be suitable as-is for most human, elven, githyanki, and tiefling recipients. You may need to adjust elements such as strap length for gnomes, dwarves, halflings, orcs, dragonborn, and goliaths.

data.name: Household Items
entries: There are few adventurers who don't appreciate the comforts of a well-appointed home, no matter how often their missions take them from its hearth. Indeed, the desire to protect their home has inspired many on their first quests and offers a joy that neither gold nor accolades can fully match. One's home is a form of everyday magic. No two are precisely alike, shaped by the needs, preferences, and whims of the folks who live there. The actual space can vary wildly, from the tiniest of nooks to grand halls suited to hosting hundreds. A home can be neat or cluttered, quiet or boisterous, an oasis of calm or a vibrant place that fills you with fresh energy. Yet in every form it is a place where you belong, exactly as you are.

Whether you are a {@class wizard} who spends most of your time ensconced in your tower's library, a {@class cleric} sharing a temple dorm with others of your faith, a {@class fighter} whose fortress stands as a bulwark against raiding hordes along a war-torn borderland, or a {@class druid} whose simple cottage blends seamlessly with its natural setting, your home reflects your sense of self and the life you've chosen. The items within that home should similarly be unique, bringing a dash of your adventures to the space itself. Charming handmade pieces will remind both yourself and your visitors of the care and effort you have put into curating your space.

The projects in this section have been chosen to suit a wide range of homes. Most blend functionality with design and whimsy. Beginners will find endless uses for the {@pattern Schools of Magic Granny Squares} and the {@pattern D20 Pillow}, whose simple design can be incorporated into a wide array of patterns as your skills grow. The {@pattern Mimic Dice Bag} will appeal to the risk-taking side of every adventurer, while challenging you with new stitching techniques. Finally, the {@pattern Soul Coin Coaster||Soul Coin Coasters} will add an infernal flair to your decor while keeping surfaces safe from unsightly stains. Each design will help contribute to a home filled with the warmth and magic that will bolster your bravery in dark moments and serve as a well-earned reprieve from danger.

items.name: bpdc:
entries: back post double crochet

items.name: bphdc:
entries: back post half double crochet

items.name: blo:
entries: back loop only

items.name: ch:
entries: chain

items.name: ch sp:
entries: chain space

items.name: dblpuff:
entries: double puff stitch

items.name: dc:
entries: double crochet

items.name: dcdec:
entries: double crochet decrease (also called dc2tog)

items.name: dcinc:
entries: double crochet increase (2dc in same stitch)

items.name: dcXtog in the same stitch:
entries: double crochet (#) stitches in the same stitch

items.name: dc3tog:
entries: double crochet 3 stitches together

items.name: dc5tog:
entries: double crochet 5 stitches together

items.name: dec:
entries: single crochet decrease (also called sc2tog)

items.name: fdc:
entries: foundation double crochet

items.name: flo:
entries: front loop only

items.name: fpdc:
entries: front post double crochet

items.name: fptr:
entries: front post treble crochet

items.name: fsc:
entries: foundation single crochet

items.name: hdc:
entries: half double crochet

items.name: hdc dec:
entries: half double crochet decrease (also called hdc2tog)

items.name: hdc inc:
entries: half double crochet increase (2hdc in same stitch)

items.name: htr:
entries: half treble crochet (U.S. version)

items.name: inc:
entries: single crochet increase (2sc in same stitch)

items.name: inv dec:
entries: invisible decrease

items.name: picot:
entries: picot stitch

items.name: popcorn:
entries: popcorn stitch

items.name: puff st:
entries: puff stitch

items.name: sc:
entries: single crochet

items.name: sk st:
entries: skip a stitch

items.name: slst:
entries: slip stitch

items.name: tr:
entries: treble crochet

items.name: Vstitch:
entries: 2dc in same stitch

items.name: 2dec in 3sc:
entries: Make a standard decrease, then start in the same stitch you just decreased into and make another decrease. This results in 2 stitches (2dec) where there were 3sc.

items.name: Back Loop Only (blo):
entries: Work through back loop of a stitch only.

items.name: Back Post Half Double Crochet (bphdc):
entries: Yarn over and insert the hook from the back to front to back around the post of the stitch of the previous row, yarn over and pull up a loop, yarn over and pull through all 3 loops on your hook.

items.name: Cable:
entries: sk 2 sts, fptr in each of next 2 sts, fptr in each of the 2 previously skipped stitches. (1 cable is 4 stitches)

items.name: Crab Stitch / Reverse Single Crochet (crab st):
entries: Ch1. Without turning your work, insert the hook from front to back in the first single crochet stitch. Yarn over your hook and draw up a loop. Yarn over and draw it through both loops.

items.name: DcXtog in the same stitch / Bobble Stitch:
entries: This is often called a "bobble stitch," but this book uses "dcXtog in the same stitch" because it allows you to vary the number of dc5tog to make smaller or larger bobbles, as needed. For example, to dc3tog in the same stitch, (1) yarn over, insert hook into stitch, yarn over and pull up a loop, yarn over and pull through two loops (leaving 2 on hook); (2) yarn over, insert hook into same stitch, yarn over and pull up a loop, yarn over and pull through 2 loops (leaving 3 on hook); (3) yarn over, insert hook into the same stitch, yarn over and pull up a loop, yarn over and pull through 2 loops, yarn over and pull through all 4 loops on hook to finish.

items.name: Double Puff Stitch (dblpuff):
entries: Yarn over, insert hook into first space and pull up a loop 2 times, yarn over and insert hook into next space 3 times, yarn over and insert hook into next space 2 times, yarn over and pull through all loops on the hook, and ch1. To work the next row above the dblpuff, work in the ch1 space. You are decreasing when you make this stitch.

items.name: Front Loop Only (flo):
entries: Work through front loop of a stitch only.

items.name: Half-Triple or Half-Treble Crochet (htr):
entries: Yarn over twice, insert hook into stitch, pull up a loop, pull through 2 loops on the hook, yarn over, pull through remaining 3 loops.

items.name: Invisible Decrease (inv dec):
entries: Insert hook into front loop of indicated stitch, insert hook into front loop of next stitch (3 loops on hook). Yarn over, pull through 2 loops. Yarn over, pull through remaining 2 loops on hook.

items.name: Picot Stitch (picot):
entries: Ch2 or 3, depending on the pattern, slst into the front loop of the last st made.

items.name: Popcorn Stitch (popcorn):
entries: 5dc in stitch indicated, remove hook from loop, insert hook into first dc made, grab loop and pull through, ch1 to finish. When working in the top of the popcorn, work in the ch1.

items.name: Puff Stitch (puff st):
entries: Yarn over, insert hook and pull up a loop 5 times, yarn over and pull through all loops on hook and ch1. To work the next row above the puff, work in the ch1 space.

items.name: Scale / Crocodile Stitch (scale):
entries: Working around the designated dc stitches, work 5dc around the post of the first dc, ch1, work 5dc around the post of the next dc (1 scale made).

items.name: Spike Stitch (spike st):
entries: This stitch is worked using the same steps as a single crochet stitch, but instead of working into the current row where you are crocheting, you drop down and work it into a row below that current row.

items.name: Vstitch:
entries: 2dc in the same stitch.

entries.name: Acknowledgments
entries: First and foremost, my heartfelt thanks to the brilliant designers without whom this book would merely be an unanchored collection of {@style Dungeons & Dragons|dnd-font} lore. The creative vision and unbridled enthusiasm that Sharon, Alysia, Rebecca, and Lydia brought to this project was a joy to experience.

My gratitude to the entire Ten Speed Press team, who brought their considerable talents to bear on this project; it's been an honor to collaborate with so many wonderful folks. Editors Claire Yee and Zoey Brandt offered expert guidance that kept the complex process rolling smoothly. Diana Drew was vital in fine-tuning my initial draft, while BJ Berti ensured the accuracy of each pattern. Betsy Stromberg developed the book's gorgeous visuals, with the added contributions of Claudia Sanchez, Abby Oladipo, and Dan Myers. Mithy Evans and the photography team, including lighting assistant Sara Bluwulf, stylist Jillian Knox, and models Claudia Sanchez, Jermaine Dante, Avery Noonan, and Erin Marshall, brought real magic to every page. Huge thanks as well to Shaida Boroumand for shepherding both this and the {@style Dungeons & Dragons|dnd-font} Young Adventurer's Guides from concept to launch.

On a personal note, I'd like to thank my parents for supporting the two interests—role-playing games and textile arts—that combined into this book. My dad took me to my first {@style Dungeons & Dragons|dnd-font} games, always encouraging my interest in fantasy and science fiction. My mom taught me to sew and was a constant source of inspiration through her quilting, needlework, and costuming projects. Their support means the world to me.
Finally, thanks to my husband, Jim Zub, for his support, and to Tory Woollcott for the steady supply of funny videos that were a crucial part of my writing process.

items.name: Stacy King
entries: is a professional nerd who has built a career out of introducing new people to her favorite obsessions. She is the coauthor of the {@style Dungeons & Dragons|dnd-font} Young Adventurer's Guide series, distilling the lore and concepts of the iconic roleplaying game for young readers. Her graphic novel work includes the Manga Classics series, which adapt Western literary classics like {@i Pride and Prejudice} and {@i Dracula}. Along with writing, she is fascinated by textile arts such as cross-stitch, needlework, sewing, and historical attire. She lives in downtown Toronto with her husband, comics author Jim Zub, and far too many dice.

items.name: Sharon Posey
entries: is based in Pensacola, Florida. She first learned crochet at the feet of her now-mother-in-law when she was just sixteen. After she and her husband had a son in 2004, she started learning how to make amigurumi. Her first one was terrible! But over time, she got better and decided she wanted to write her own patterns. She started simple and worked her way up to detailed designs. Now, Sharon and her husband run Posey Handmade together.

items.name: Alysia Sly
entries: is a fiber artist based in Sioux Falls, South Dakota. She started crocheting around age nine in the early 2000s, thanks to lessons from her aunt and grandma, and some of her first projects were dresses for her dolls. During college, she revived her love of crochet when she discovered amigurumi, and she's been crocheting nonstop ever since. She opened her online shop, Co-op Creations SD, in late 2021. When she's not crocheting, she loves spending time with her husband, Josh, and their two cats, Ajani and Tami.

items.name: Rebecca Stone
entries: is a social scientist and fiber artist born in Sydney, Australia, and now based in Chelmsford, Massachusetts. She started crocheting in January 2021 as an alternative to doomscrolling and quickly fell in love with fantasy-inspired amigurumi. She now owns BextraOrdinary Creatures, where she enjoys designing fantasy crochet patterns and making eyes, horns, and other accessories to bring her creations to life. In addition to crochet, Rebecca loves spending time with her partner, Jason, and their two sons, Cameron and Caleb, who are excellent creative collaborators.

items.name: Lydia Viatoris
entries: is a teacher, artist, and designer living in Jacksonville, Florida. She first learned how to crochet from her high school math teacher as a way to pass the time after exams, and she has continued to pursue fiber arts ever since. Lydia has a career teaching high school natural sciences and has completed a masters degree in biology. She has been applying the scientific method of hypothesis and experimentation to her crochet work, creating patterns under the name EarthTricks since 2014. She has a great love of learning, trying new things, and general tomfoolery in the creative realm.
